items.q: How do you say 'I travel by train' in Spanish?
items.why: Students use por or con. Transport always uses EN: en coche, en avión, en autobús, en tren, en barco. Exception: a pie (on foot), a caballo (on horseback). Critical holiday/travel vocab.

items.q: When do you use the imperfect rather than the preterite?
items.correct: For ongoing, habitual, or descriptive past actions.
items.misconception: When the action happened a long time ago.
items.random_wrong: When you're unsure of the exact date.
items.why: Students choose tense by recency. The distinction is ASPECTUAL: preterite = completed/single ('comí' I ate); imperfect = ongoing/habitual ('comía' I used to eat / I was eating).

items.q: Which Spanish 'you' do you use with a stranger or elderly person?
items.why: Students use tú with everyone like English 'you'. Spanish distinguishes FORMAL (usted) for politeness/distance and INFORMAL (tú) for friends/family. Usted takes third-person verb forms, which trips students up.
Correct answer: Bunuel. “Almodovar” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which Cuaron film won 2019 Oscars including Best Director?
items.why: It's Roma. “Y tu mama tambien” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year was Un perro andaluz made?
items.why: Exact answer: 1929. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
